ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=2] A(2): The work applies off-the-shelf supervised learning (Random Forests) and standard surface/semantic features (keyword-fit measures and cosine similarity) to a new application (Japanese national-center short-answer social-studies tests) rather than proposing a new algorithmic idea. The dataset is small (8 items from Gakken FY2015) and the main novelty appears to be task-specific engineering (e.g., marking mandatory content words and producing recommended-score probabilities) rather than non-trivial methodological advances. Given these facts, the contribution mainly combines known techniques with limited novelty beyond their application context. | B(2): The paper applies well-established techniques (Random Forests, cosine-similarity semantic features, surface keyword matching) to Japanese social-studies short-answer scoring, so the core methodology is not new. Its main differences are an application to a new national-center-style dataset, the practical feature of marking mandatory words/phrases, and outputting recommended-score probabilities, which are useful but incremental. The authors also report negative results (case-grammar features ineffective) and note limitations on deep semantic items, reinforcing that no novel modelling approach was introduced. Overall this is largely a combination of known methods with limited novelty.

reviewer_a.justification: The work applies off-the-shelf supervised learning (Random Forests) and standard surface/semantic features (keyword-fit measures and cosine similarity) to a new application (Japanese national-center short-answer social-studies tests) rather than proposing a new algorithmic idea. The dataset is small (8 items from Gakken FY2015) and the main novelty appears to be task-specific engineering (e.g., marking mandatory content words and producing recommended-score probabilities) rather than non-trivial methodological advances. Given these facts, the contribution mainly combines known techniques with limited novelty beyond their application context.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper applies well-established techniques (Random Forests, cosine-similarity semantic features, surface keyword matching) to Japanese social-studies short-answer scoring, so the core methodology is not new. Its main differences are an application to a new national-center-style dataset, the practical feature of marking mandatory words/phrases, and outputting recommended-score probabilities, which are useful but incremental. The authors also report negative results (case-grammar features ineffective) and note limitations on deep semantic items, reinforcing that no novel modelling approach was introduced. Overall this is largely a combination of known methods with limited novelty.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=2] A(2): The paper only evaluates against human ratings as the baseline and does not empirically compare the Random Forest system to other automated short-answer scoring methods or recent machine-learning baselines. While it reports sensible metrics (exact match, within-one-point) and analyzes feature effectiveness (e.g., case-grammar found ineffective) and item-level failures, key comparative baselines are missing so the comparisons are inadequate. The summary notes related work coverage is adequate but no evidence is given that prior methods were tested or that the paper acknowledges when prior approaches perform comparably or better. | B(3): The paper only benchmarks its system against human ratings rather than against other automated short-answer scoring methods or recent ML baselines, so important empirical baselines are missing. Comparisons to human raters are conducted on the same trial data and metrics (so the setup is fair), and the authors discuss limitations and related work. However, without empirical comparison to prior automated approaches the evaluation is incomplete.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper only evaluates against human ratings as the baseline and does not empirically compare the Random Forest system to other automated short-answer scoring methods or recent machine-learning baselines. While it reports sensible metrics (exact match, within-one-point) and analyzes feature effectiveness (e.g., case-grammar found ineffective) and item-level failures, key comparative baselines are missing so the comparisons are inadequate. The summary notes related work coverage is adequate but no evidence is given that prior methods were tested or that the paper acknowledges when prior approaches perform comparably or better.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper only benchmarks its system against human ratings rather than against other automated short-answer scoring methods or recent ML baselines, so important empirical baselines are missing. Comparisons to human raters are conducted on the same trial data and metrics (so the setup is fair), and the authors discuss limitations and related work. However, without empirical comparison to prior automated approaches the evaluation is incomplete.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The experiments provide an initial, task-focused evaluation (8 real test items, detailed performance numbers including per-item failure on deep-semantic item) and limitations are explicitly discussed (entailment, data skew, case-grammar ineffectiveness). However, the evaluation is narrow (only 8 items from one trial exam), there are no reported ablation or sensitivity analyses of feature sets or model choices, and generalization to broader item types is untested. Related work coverage is described as adequate and hyperparameters are reported, but reproducibility is limited (no code mentioned) and larger/more diverse experiments are needed to fully support the claims. | B(3): The experiments provide useful evidence (exact-match and within-one-point rates on the Gakken FY2015 trial set) and the paper candidly discusses failure modes (poor performance on deep-semantic items, skew from many zeros, and ineffective case-grammar features). However, the evaluation is limited to only eight test items and there is no mention of ablation or sensitivity analyses to quantify the contribution of different predictor types. Related work coverage is stated as adequate, but reproducibility is only partial (hyperparameters reported but code and an accessible dataset are not mentioned). Overall the study is adequate but misses important experiments and analyses to fully support its claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments provide an initial, task-focused evaluation (8 real test items, detailed performance numbers including per-item failure on deep-semantic item) and limitations are explicitly discussed (entailment, data skew, case-grammar ineffectiveness). Overall the study is adequate but misses important experiments and analyses to fully support its claims.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper's main claims (Random Forests using surface and semantic predictors achieve high within-one-point agreement for many items) are supported by reported experimental results (70–95% for several items, but 48% on a deep-semantic item) but the evidence is limited: dataset covers only 8 test items from one trial exam and skewness (many zero scores) is acknowledged as degrading performance. There are no mathematical derivations to evaluate, and the experimental methodology lacks detail on statistical significance testing, wider baselines, or cross-validation procedures, which raises concerns about generalizability. Overall the conclusions are partially justified by the presented results but stronger empirical validation is needed to fully support the claims. | B(3): The empirical claims are partially supported by the reported experiments (e.g., 70–95% within-one accuracy on items not requiring deep semantics and 48% on a deep-semantic item), and the use of Random Forests with surface and cosine-similarity predictors is a reasonable implementation. There are no mathematical derivations to check, and the negative result about case-grammar predictors is consistent with the report. However, the experimental methodology is limited: only eight test items from a single source, no alternative model baselines or ablation analyses reported, and no statistical significance testing, which weakens generalization of the conclusions. The authors do acknowledge major limitations, so the conclusions are tentatively justified but further evidence is needed for stronger claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claims (Random Forests using surface and semantic predictors achieve high within-one-point agreement for many items) are supported by reported experimental results (70–95% for several items, but 48% on a deep-semantic item) but the evidence is limited: dataset covers only 8 test items from one trial exam and skewness (many zero scores) is acknowledged as degrading performance. There are no mathematical derivations to evaluate, and the experimental methodology lacks detail on statistical significance testing, wider baselines, or cross-validation procedures, which raises concerns about generalizability. Overall the conclusions are partially justified by the presented results but stronger empirical validation is needed to fully support the claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The empirical claims are partially supported by the reported experiments (e.g., 70–95% within-one accuracy on items not requiring deep semantics and 48% on a deep-semantic item), and the use of Random Forests with surface and cosine-similarity predictors is a reasonable implementation. There are no mathematical derivations to check, and the negative result about case-grammar predictors is consistent with the report. However, the experimental methodology is limited: only eight test items from a single source, no alternative model baselines or ablation analyses reported, and no statistical significance testing, which weakens generalization of the conclusions. The authors do acknowledge major limitations, so the conclusions are tentatively justified but further evidence is needed for stronger claims.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=2] A(2): The topic (automated short-answer scoring) is within NLP/ML scope, but the work as summarized is an applied system using standard Random Forests and surface/semantic similarity features on a very small evaluation set (8 items from one trial), with limited handling of deep semantic phenomena. That combination—modest methodological novelty, limited dataset size, and primarily applied system evaluation—makes it a better fit for an applied/educational-technology venue than a top-tier NLP/ML conference. | B(3): The topic—automated short-answer scoring using NLP/ML techniques—is within the scope of a top NLP/ML conference and would interest its audience (summary shows use of semantic similarity, feature engineering, and supervised learning). However, the work appears application-focused with relatively standard methods (Random Forests, cosine similarity) and a very limited evaluation set (only 8 test items from one trial exam), which weakens its fit for a top-tier venue expecting larger-scale experiments or novel modeling contributions. Given adequate related-work coverage but clear limitations around deep semantic understanding and data skew, this paper is borderline appropriate and might be better suited to a specialized education-assessment or applied NLP venue unless expanded.

reviewer_a.justification: The topic (automated short-answer scoring) is within NLP/ML scope, but the work as summarized is an applied system using standard Random Forests and surface/semantic similarity features on a very small evaluation set (8 items from one trial), with limited handling of deep semantic phenomena. That combination—modest methodological novelty, limited dataset size, and primarily applied system evaluation—makes it a better fit for an applied/educational-technology venue than a top-tier NLP/ML conference.

reviewer_b.justification: The topic—automated short-answer scoring using NLP/ML techniques—is within the scope of a top NLP/ML conference and would interest its audience (summary shows use of semantic similarity, feature engineering, and supervised learning). However, the work appears application-focused with relatively standard methods (Random Forests, cosine similarity) and a very limited evaluation set (only 8 test items from one trial exam), which weakens its fit for a top-tier venue expecting larger-scale experiments or novel modeling contributions. Given adequate related-work coverage but clear limitations around deep semantic understanding and data skew, this paper is borderline appropriate and might be better suited to a specialized education-assessment or applied NLP venue unless expanded.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper addresses an important applied problem—automating Japanese short-answer scoring for national center tests—by building a practical system using Random Forests with surface-level and semantic (cosine similarity) predictors. Empirical results show useful performance for many items (roughly 70–95% within-one-point agreement) but substantial failure on items requiring deep semantic understanding (48% on one World History item), and the approach depends on sizable, well-balanced human-scored training data. Given the limited dataset (8 items), standard modeling choices, and explicit limitations on textual entailment, the work offers a moderate-impact, practitioner-relevant contribution that can be built upon but is unlikely to be transformative. | B(3): The paper tackles an important, practical problem—automating short-answer scoring for high-stakes Japanese national tests—and demonstrates a working system (Random Forests with surface and semantic predictors, mandatory-keyword marking) that achieves human-like agreement (≈70–95% within one point on many items). However, evaluation is limited (only 8 items from a single trial dataset), performance degrades sharply for items requiring deep semantic understanding (48% within one point), and the approach relies on substantial labeled data and standard methods. Given these strengths and clear limitations, the contribution is a useful, moderately impactful applied result that is unlikely to be transformative but will be valuable to practitioners and spur incremental follow-up research.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an important applied problem—automating Japanese short-answer scoring for national center tests—by building a practical system using Random Forests with surface-level and semantic (cosine similarity) predictors. Empirical results show useful performance for many items (roughly 70–95% within-one-point agreement) but substantial failure on items requiring deep semantic understanding (48% on one World History item), and the approach depends on sizable, well-balanced human-scored training data. Given the limited dataset (8 items), standard modeling choices, and explicit limitations on textual entailment, the work offers a moderate-impact, practitioner-relevant contribution that can be built upon but is unlikely to be transformative.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles an important, practical problem—automating short-answer scoring for high-stakes Japanese national tests—and demonstrates a working system (Random Forests with surface and semantic predictors, mandatory-keyword marking) that achieves human-like agreement (≈70–95% within one point on many items). However, evaluation is limited (only 8 items from a single trial dataset), performance degrades sharply for items requiring deep semantic understanding (48% within one point), and the approach relies on substantial labeled data and standard methods. Given these strengths and clear limitations, the contribution is a useful, moderately impactful applied result that is unlikely to be transformative but will be valuable to practitioners and spur incremental follow-up research.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well organized with a clear methodology-to-experiments presentation (Random Forest approach, predictors, datasets, evaluation metrics and limitations are all reported). The main contribution — an automated Japanese short-answer scoring/support system and its performance characteristics — is stated early and repeatedly. Notation and formalism are not highlighted in the summary (hyperparameters are reported but there is no mention of detailed or problematic notation), and figures/tables are not described even though concrete example items (e.g. World History B2 #3, Japanese History B1 #2) are used; these are minor presentation gaps. Overall the writing is described as adequate, so the paper is well-written with only small clarity/presentation issues. | B(4): The paper is generally well organized and reads logically from method to experiments and conclusion: the supervised Random Forest approach, predictors, dataset (Gakken FY2015) and evaluation metrics are clearly described, and hyperparameters are reported. The main contributions and limitations are stated up front (automated scoring system, semantic vs. surface predictors, and failure cases such as World History B2 #3), and concrete examples/metrics (70–95% within-one-point, 48% for a deep-semantic item) make results easy to follow. Notation and methodological detail appear consistent and sufficient for reproduction, and writing quality is characterized as adequate. Minor presentation issues remain (no paper title provided in the summary, and it is unclear whether figures/tables are present or fully informative), so I rate it a 4.

The main contributions and limitations are stated up front (automated scoring system, semantic vs. surface predictors, and failure cases such as World History B2 #3), and concrete examples/metrics (70–95% within-one-point, 48% for a deep-semantic item) make results easy to follow. Notation and methodological detail appear consistent and sufficient for reproduction, and writing quality is characterized as adequate. Minor presentation issues remain (no paper title provided in the summary, and it is unclear whether figures/tables are present or fully informative), so I rate it a 4.

main_claims: Developed an automated Japanese short-answer scoring and support system for new national center written tests (short-answer scoring for social studies).

Used Random Forests with both surface-level (keyword/fitness) and semantic (cosine similarity) predictors to generate automated scores and recommended score probabilities, with human raters making final judgments.

On a trial set (8 social-studies test items from the Gakken nation-wide trial examination FY2015) the automated scores matched human ratings within one point for roughly 70–95% of cases when high-level semantic judgment was not required (notably lower, 48%, for an item requiring deep semantic understanding).

Implemented functionality to mark content-like important words/phrases as 'mandatory' (and reduce score if missing); found case-grammar agreement to be ineffective as a predictor and thus did not use it.
_summary.methodology: Built a supervised prediction model using Random Forests (RandomForest package in R, default procedure of 500 trees) with predictors including degree-of-fit measures and semantic (cosine) similarity between model answers, written answers, and test-item sentences; the system outputs automated scores and recommended-score probabilities which are then reviewed by human raters.
datasets: Gakken nation-wide trial examination (fiscal year 2015) — 8 test items across World History, Japanese History, and Geography
metrics: exact match rate (prediction identical to correct answer)
probability that automated estimate differed from human rating by within one point
limitations: Recognizing textual entailment and deep semantic equivalence remains difficult; system performs poorly on items requiring deep semantic understanding (example: World History B2 #3).

Requires a sufficiently large and well-balanced set of human-scored examples for supervised learning; skewed data (many zero scores) degrades machine learning performance (example: Japanese History B1 #2).
Agreement of case-grammar elements (deep cases) was rare and not an effective predictor, so case-grammar variables were not used.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - automated short-answer scoring for educational assessment
